claims.text: In October 1957, three Royal Navy cadets — William Lang, Michael Crowley, and Ray Baker — on a map-reading exercise in Suffolk, England, descended into the village of Kersey and allegedly walked into a medieval time slip before running out.

evidence.quote:
Suffolk, England, October 1957.
Three Royal Navy cadets walked across
green hills on a training exercise, map
reading and orientation. William Lang,
Michael Crowley, and Ray Baker

claims.rationale: The 'Kersey time slip' is a documented SPR case file (Andrew MacKenzie, 'Adventures in Time', 1997). Three cadets' written accounts decades later exist; Kersey's real history matches some details (plague, halted church tower). Whether the experience was a literal time slip versus shared misperception, hallucination, or later narrative embellishment is unknown. directTruth below 0.5 because the core supernatural interpretation has no physical evidence; only witness testimony.

claims.text: Psychical researcher Andrew McKenzie, after decades of cross-referencing Lang's memories with historical records, concluded the cadets witnessed Kersey as it would have appeared around 1420 — 70 years after the Black Death reached the village in 1350.

evidence.quote:
McKenzie concluded the
cadets saw Courcey as it was around
1420, 70 years after the plague

claims.rationale: MacKenzie's research and 1420 dating appear in his SPR publications. The factual match (glazed windows appear post-1400, church tower halted in 1348, wool trade recovery) is clever storytelling. However, MacKenzie's conclusion is an interpretation of a single witness narrative decades after the fact — not independent corroboration that the cadets saw anything supernatural. The historical-alignment argument is suggestive but also the kind of thing that confirmation bias produces naturally.

claims.text: The Kersey church tower construction was halted in 1348, and the half-built tower stood unfinished for decades — a specific architectural fact that matches the cadets' description of a half-built church.
claims.rationale: The 1348 Black Death halt of Kersey's St Mary's church tower construction is verifiable from local church records and historical surveys — the village was severely affected. The suggestive alignment with the cadets' account is the strongest piece of 'explained by real history' corroboration, but a cadet on a 1957 orientation exercise could plausibly have read local history beforehand.

claims.text: The Black Death that reached Kersey in 1350 killed approximately half of England's population — a demographic catastrophe the host uses to frame the village's allegedly remembered trauma.

evidence.quote:
1350 was the year of Black Death reached
Courcey.
The plague killed half of England.

claims.rationale: The Black Death (1348-1350 in England) killed an estimated 40-60% of the English population — standard estimate in Benedictow's 'The Black Death' and most plague historiography. Half-of-England figure is within the accepted range. The Kersey-1350 date is plausible; the plague moved through East Anglia in 1349-50.

claims.text: In February 1983, during a temporary furlough from Monroe County Correctional Facility to attend his abusive grandfather's funeral, Don Decker became the center of a water-materialization and telekinetic phenomenon witnessed by multiple police, corrections officers, and a chaplain.

evidence.quote:
Don Decker served time at Monroe County
Correctional Facility for receiving
stolen property. Don was 21 years old.
The stolen property charge was minor,
tools taken from a construction site. He
got 18 months.
[Music]
In February 1983, he got a temporary
furlough to attend his grandfather's
funeral.

claims.rationale: The 'Rain Boy of Stroudsburg' case is documented. Don Decker was real; he served time at Monroe County; the February 1983 incident produced signed witness statements from Officer John Baujan, Officer Ron Van Why, Lieutenant Keenhold, etc. The case was featured on 'Unsolved Mysteries' (1993 season). Whether the events were paranormal or elaborate collective misperception/shared fraud is unresolved.

claims.text: During the prison incident, multiple officers watched three parallel scratch marks appear on Don Decker's neck and cheeks in real time with no visible source, and a gold cross placed in his hand reportedly burned him.

evidence.quote:
Three deep scratches appeared on his
neck

claims.rationale: The scratch claims are in the officers' statements and the 'Unsolved Mysteries' episode. The scratches appearing 'in real time' with multiple witnesses is consistent with accounts; whether the marks were pre-existing and missed, self-inflicted in a dissociative state, or genuinely anomalous is open. directTruth middling — witness consistency supports that officers saw marks; supernatural cause remains unsupported.

claims.text: Parapsychologists describe Decker's case as 'recurrent spontaneous psychokinesis' (RSPK) — a theoretical trauma-triggered category documented over 100 times in the field's literature.

evidence.quote:
Parapsychologists have a name for this,
recurrent spontaneous psychokinesis,
RSPK.

claims.rationale: RSPK is a real diagnostic category within parapsychology (William G. Roll, 'The Poltergeist', 1972), proposing that adolescent/trauma-driven psychokinesis explains classical poltergeist cases. The '100+ documented cases' count reflects parapsychology literature, which mainstream science does not accept as providing evidence of genuine psychokinesis. directTruth: the definition is correctly reported; the implied existence of the phenomenon is not scientifically established.

claims.text: On April 5, 2015, a YouTube channel named Unfavorable Semicircle began uploading, posting 1,247 videos within the first 24 hours — a volume and rate impossible on ordinary YouTube uploader pipelines.

evidence.quote:
April 5th, 2015, a
YouTube channel appeared with a nonsense
name, Unfavorable Semicircle. That first
day, that channel uploaded 1,247
videos within 24 hours.

claims.rationale: Unfavorable Semicircle is a well-documented internet mystery. The April 5, 2015 start date and first-day volume match the Reddit archival records and news coverage (BBC Future, Feb 25 2016 article). Upload patterns were logged by investigators in the r/UnfavorableSemicircle subreddit.

claims.text: Within nine months the Unfavorable Semicircle channel posted over 72,000 videos at a peak rate of three videos per minute, requiring direct server access or backend permissions not normally granted to YouTube creators.

evidence.quote:
Within
9 months, it posted over 72,000 videos.

claims.rationale: Total count (~72,000+) and peak rate (~3/min) are widely cited in contemporary coverage and Reddit archives. Whether this 'required backend permissions' or simply exploited a bug / undocumented API endpoint is not established — the host's inference is plausible but not proven; small cloud scripts operating against YouTube's 2015-era Data API could sustain high upload rates before rate limiting was tightened.

claims.text: BBC Future published an article on February 25, 2016 calling Unfavorable Semicircle 'YouTube's strangest mystery'; within hours YouTube suspended the channel, citing terms-of-service spam violations.

evidence.quote:
On February 25th, 2016, the DBC
published an article calling it
YouTube's strangest mystery.

claims.rationale: The BBC Future article by Brad Esposito (transcript says 'DBC' — likely typo) on Feb 25 2016 exists and described the channel in exactly that framing. YouTube's suspension followed shortly after. The temporal correlation is documented. Verifiable via Wayback Machine archives of the BBC article.

claims.text: Combined-frame analysis of Unfavorable Semicircle's 'lock' video series produced an image confirmed by the creator in 2022 to be the Voyager Golden Record — humanity's 1977 message to aliens — implying the channel was responding to that message.

evidence.quote:
the Voyager
Golden Record, the message humanity sent
to aliens in 1977.

claims.rationale: The Voyager 1 and 2 launches (1977) and the Golden Record (Sagan, Drake, Druyan) are real. The claim that combined frames form the Golden Record image is reported by Reddit investigators; whether the composite is compelling or a pareidolic match is subjective. The 'creator confirmed in 2022 via BrickSails on Twitter' part is reported by the host but the broader 2022 Twitter claim ('outsider art project') undercuts the elaborate-meaning interpretation.

claims.text: In 2022 an account named 'BrickSails' on Twitter claimed to be the Unfavorable Semicircle creator and said the entire channel was an outsider-art project aimed at uploading the most videos ever to see if people would watch.

evidence.quote:
In 2022,
someone claiming to be the creator of
BrickSails on Twitter.
He said it was an outsider art project.

claims.rationale: [alternative] The 2022 BrickSails attribution is reported in online discussions of the mystery. If true, it is the most mundane explanation — artistic curiosity at scale. The host characterizes this as incomplete (doesn't explain coordinates, real-time adaptation, etc.), but an unpretentious outsider-art claim from the originator is by default the simplest hypothesis and should carry substantial prior weight vs. 'alien intelligence' alternatives.

claims.text: When engineers traced the upload source of Unfavorable Semicircle, they allegedly found it led to a data center in Iceland — equipment that had been offline for months, according to the host.

evidence.quote:
the upload source, it led to a
data center in Iceland.

claims.rationale: [logical] The 'traced to offline Iceland data center' claim is common in creepypasta embellishments of real mysteries. No public YouTube engineering disclosure of such a trace exists. The claim is internally contradictory (an offline server cannot be the source of active uploads) and unverifiable — the host clearly flags this as campfire storytelling material.

claims.text: The host frames the entire episode as a 'campfire story' explicitly told without debunking or analysis, intended to entertain rather than evaluate — a framing that affects how each story's truth claims should be weighted.
claims.rationale: The host's own closing framing — a rare explicit disclaimer. Not arguing truth so much as telling three linked uncanny stories for entertainment. Makes directTruth reasonable to set high for the meta-framing claim, even while directTruth on the underlying stories varies widely.
